name: Deny self and take up the cross
notes: Immediately after Peter's confession and the first passion prediction, all three synoptics record the same teaching on the cost of discipleship: deny self, take up the cross, and follow. The shared core runs verse for verse through four sayings: the call to follow, the paradox of saving and losing one's life, the worthlessness of gaining the world while forfeiting the soul, and the warning that the Son of man comes in his Father's glory with the holy angels. Each account keeps its own vantage: Luke adds that the cross is taken up 'daily,' Mark anchors losing one's life 'for my sake and the gospel's' in 'this adulterous and sinful generation,' and Matthew alone closes with the Son of man rewarding 'every man according to his works' rather than the 'ashamed' saying. Matthew folds the soul's profit and the soul's ransom into a single verse where Mark spreads them across two, and Matthew speaks of the coming in glory while Mark and Luke join it to being ashamed of Christ. These are complementary vantages of one sermon, each preserving detail the others leave in place.
